course.name: Operating Systems
course.studentLevel: Upper-division CS majors with systems programming experience
course.assessmentFormat: GitHub Classroom lab and in-class activities

lecture.summary: Introduce the motivation for virtual memory, explain page translation, and connect paging tradeoffs to performance and protection.
lecture.openingHook: Start with a process that appears to own a large contiguous address space even though physical memory is fragmented and shared.
objectives: Explain why virtual memory exists and what problems it solves.
Trace address translation through the page table and TLB.
Evaluate paging tradeoffs involving locality, overhead, and protection.
caseStudies: Single-level page table overhead on 32-bit systems
TLB misses during a memory-intensive workload
activities: Walk through one address translation by hand.
Ask students to predict whether a workload will improve or destroy locality.
takeaways: Paging is an illusion layer that trades space and translation overhead for flexibility and protection.
The TLB matters because the page table is too expensive to consult for every access.
Locality is the difference between a practical VM system and a collapsing one.
discussionQuestions: When is a larger page size a net win?
How should an OS respond when a process begins to thrash?
Which part of paging is most visible to an application developer?
references: Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces, virtualization chapters
Architecture notes on TLB structure and page walks

sections.title: Why Virtual Memory Exists
sections.overview: Frame the abstraction: each process sees a clean private address space while the OS manages sharing and fragmentation underneath.
points: Isolation and protection are easier with per-process virtual address spaces.
Physical memory allocation is too constrained to expose directly to user processes.
The abstraction allows programs to be written without knowing physical placement.

callouts.label: KEY
callouts.text: Virtual memory is not just a capacity trick; it is a protection and management mechanism.

speakerNotes: Use a fragmented RAM diagram and contrast it with the clean process view.

blanks.template: Each process sees a private _______ address space while the OS maps it onto physical memory.

sections.title: Page Translation and the TLB
sections.overview: Break down address translation, page table lookup, and why TLB locality determines whether paging is cheap or painful.
points: A virtual address splits into page number and offset.
The TLB caches recent translations to avoid repeated page table walks.
TLB misses amplify the real cost of memory references.
activities: Translate one sample address using a small page table.

sections.title: Page Faults, Replacement, and Thrashing
sections.overview: Connect VM policy decisions to runtime behavior by showing page faults, replacement pressure, and collapse under poor locality.
points: A page fault transfers control to the OS when the needed page is absent.
Replacement policy tries to keep the working set resident.
Thrashing happens when the fault rate is high enough that useful progress stalls.
activities: Have students classify workloads as locality-friendly or locality-hostile.

blanks.template: When the working set does not fit and the fault rate explodes, the system begins _______.

questions.prompt: Trace a virtual address through the paging system and explain where a TLB hit changes the cost profile.
questions.answer: A strong answer identifies VPN, offset, TLB lookup, page-table fallback, and resulting physical address.

questions.prompt: Explain why thrashing occurs and name one mitigation the OS can apply.
questions.answer: A strong answer connects working-set pressure to excessive page faults and proposes a defensible mitigation.
